2: The interest in anaerobic digestion (AD) and biogas production technology has grown rapidly over the years (Poeschl et al., 2010), mainly due to the increasing importance of renewable energy deployment with respect to structured mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (BMU, 2007) and the need for sustainable management of organic waste (BMU, 2002). The number of biogas plants in Germany has increased by about 90% (2300 plants) in the last 5 years, with an average capacity of 500 kWel compared to 60 kWel in 1999 (Olzem, 2010). The introduction of economic incentives aimed at enhancing the deployment of renewable energies via the Renewable Energy Sources Act (EEG) has led to the proliferation of biogas production from large-scale plants organized in industrial parks, and with elaborate feedstock logistics. The required intensive energy crop cultivation to supply the feedstock to the biogas plants, coupled with concerns over increased traffic volume associated with feedstock supply logistics, has raised pertinent concerns over environmental compatibility of biogas production in such industrial-scale parks. Material and energy are consumed in feedstock supply chains, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization, and in digestate handling and secondary processing into organic fertilizer as a by-product. Therefore, integrated assessment of environmental impacts of biogas deployment through viable conversion pathways is neces- sary for evaluation of the system processes, specifically to locate where overall environmental sustainability could be further enhanced. The biogas industry and biogas technology researchers are aware of the importance of cleaner biogas production and utiliza- tion pathways, and environmental impacts mitigation strategies based on whole-life assessment techniques (Lunghi et al., 2004; Edelmann et al., 2001; Börjesson and Berglund, 2007). For example, one of the first assessments of the potential ecological impacts of biogas production by LCA was carried out by Edelmann et al. (2001) for the Swiss Federal Office of Energy, in a study that was limited to agricultural biogas plants. Hartmann (2006) carried out a detailed LCA on industrial-scale biogas production systems that digested mainly energy crops and manure, geared to electricity generation in large-scale CHP generation and fuel cells. The study by Börjesson and Berglund (2006) analyzed the process from feedstock type to biogas utilization, including various scenarios for digestate handling. However, they did not consider a range of innovative technologies that have become available for deployment of biogas, e.g., fuel cell technology, Stirling engine, Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) and micro gas turbine (Poeschl et al., in press). In most part, the foci of the studies concerned with environmental impacts analyses have given generalized assessments of renewable energy resources (Varun and Prakash, 2009; Uihlein et al., 2008) or specific feedstocks and biogas plants (Cherubini and Ulgiati, 2010; Smyth et al., 2009; Jury et al., 2010), encompassed only one or a few unit processes in biogas production chain (Chevalier and Meunier, 2005; Pertl et al., 2010) and also applied different environmental impact analysis methodologies (Jury et al., 2010; Pertl et al., 2010; Eriksson et al., 2005), hence, the outcomes may not be objectively compared. Rigorous assessment of energy efficiency and environ- mental performance of biogas systems requires detailed assess- ment of biogas production from multiple feedstock and energy conversion scenarios, including options for utilization and/or disposal of the final digestate. This study conducted an attributional Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of multiple biogas production and utilization pathways against specific base scenarios. The objective was to rate the envi- ronmental performance of the individual systems and to identify areas where further mitigation of potential environmental impacts

could be realized to enhance environmental sustainability of biogas deployment. Analyses of case scenarios that best describe the potential variations in biogas production and utilization were undertaken to establish the effects of a number of parameters, including: (i) single feedstock digestion; (ii) co-digestion of multiple feedstocks in small (<500 kWel) and large-scale (>500 kWe1) biogas plants; (iii) different biogas utilization path- ways, and; (iv) digestate management options. This paper, which presents the second part of the analysis of the environmental impacts of biogas deployment, proceeds from the findings from an earlier study by the authors (Poeschl et al., in press), in which the unit processes with greatest potential for emission reduction were identified via compilation of a detailed Life Cycle Inventory (LCI).

4: The biogas systems considered four unit processes (Fig. 1), namely: the feedstock supply logistics; biogas production in small (<500 kWe1) and large-scale (>500 plants; biogas utilization, and; the digestate processing and handling. The analysis consid- ered potential variations in: feedstock type, including, single (A) and multiple (B) feedstock digestion; biogas utilization pathways (C), and; the digestate processing and handling (D). The inter- process transportation between feedstock supply and biogas production, and digestate processing and handling assumed material transportation over distances within 5 km radi.

The base scenarios were defined and notated on the basis of the feedstock regime. For single feedstock, the base case considered the use of cattle manure feedstock. For multiple feedstock scenarios, the base case for small-scale biogas plants (Base SS) and large-scale plants (Base LS) considered the co-digestion of cattle manure-corn silage mixture, and cattle manure-MSW-residues of food industry, respectively. The respective scenarios represent typical pathways in biogas production and utilization for small and large-scale plants in Germany (Fig. 1). The specified base scenarios provided the datum for evaluation of the environmental and health impacts attributed to the different conversion pathways considered in the study. The analysis considered potential variations in: feedstock type, including, (A) single (Appendix - Table A1) and (B) multiple (Appendix - Table A2) feedstock digestion; (C) biogas utilization pathways (Appendix - Table A3), and; (D) the digestate processing and handling (Appendix - Table A4).

6: Evaluation criteria and methods that can reliably assess the potential environmental sustainability of renewable energy system deployment is prerequisite for selection of the optimal alternative and informing decision-makers of the integrated performances of the alternatives, including the monitoring of impact on the social environment. A sustainable energy system balances energy production and consumption, with minimal negative impact on the environment, coupled with inherent opportunities for imple- mentation of social and economic activities (Hofman and Li, 2009). Wang et al. (2009) outlined the appropriate multi-criteria decision- making procedure for sustainable energy systems to include: (i) technology (primary energy ratio, etc.); (ii) economy (investment costs, etc.); (iii) environment (CO2 emissions, etc.), and; (iv) society (job creation, etc.). In this study the potential environmental impacts of biogas production and utilization pathways (Section 2.2.1) were evaluated vis-à-vis earlier results by the authors on technology assessment of identical case scenarios (Pöschl et al., 2010) (Section 2.2.2), and economic and social considerations

underpinning sustainable biogas production and utilization (Poeschl et al., 2010).

The evaluation of the environmental impacts, hence, sustain- ability of a technological option requires an LCA of a product or system, i.e., from raw material acquisition to final disposal (from cradle to grave), usually in four successive stages of analyses, including: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI); (iii) Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA), and; (iv) interpre- tation of the results (Contreras et al., 2009). Outcomes of the detailed LCI analysis for the systems under consideration in this study have been reported in Poeschl et al. in press. This paper therefore focused on the LCIA and interpretation of the results following the LCA study principles as specified in the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The analyses were done by attributional LCA, to understand the environmental impacts directly associated with the life-cycle (EC, 2010), using SimaPro LCA software (Pré Consultants, 2009).

2.2.1.1. Goal and scope definition. The main goal was to conduct an LCIA to enable a comparison of environmental and health impacts (viz., damage to human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability) of different biogas production and utilization path- ways, against the declared base scenarios. The LCIA was to provide the basis for identification of pathway-dependent environmental burdens, for possible improvement of associated technologies and processes in order to enhance the environmental sustainability of biogas as a renewable energy resource. In such context, the results inform about environmental compatible technologies for biogas production and utilization. The analyses covered the environmental and public health impacts associated with the entire biogas production chain outlined in Fig. 1. Sensitivity analysis was done to quantify the potential impacts of: (i) feedstock and digestate transportation range, (ii) variation in biogas yield in MSW, and; (iii)
electricity supply source in biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics.

Biogas plants currently rely on alternative feedstock from food processing and MSW streams, due to their easy availability, and also high cost of alternative energy crop feedstock. High feedstock demand has led to reduction in gate fees, therefore biogas plants are more attractive for management of organic waste (Poeschl et al., 2010). The main process in biogas production system is the AD of organic material for biogas production, which, after cleaning and secondary processing, could be used to generate electricity and heat, and in direct applications as substitute for natural gas or as transportation fuel. The functional unit applied in the assessment was the treatment of 1 tonne of organic material (feedstock) in small and large-scale biogas plants. The biogas generated was considered as main product, which contributes to the total envi- ronmental load. The by-products were included by system expan- sion (Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011), hence, digestate and energy derived from biogas, were considered to substitute other products in the market. The system boundary (Fig. 1) considered the production of chemical fertilizers, electricity generation with Ger- many's fuel mix, heat generation using natural gas, as well as imports of natural gas and transportation fuel. Their equivalent environmental loads were subtracted from the scenario's total environmental impacts.

2.2.1.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI). The LCI involved the compilation and quantification of inputs and outputs, for biogas production and utilization throughout the product life-cycle (ISO, 2006a). A number of different data sources were used to generate a system- atic inventory of energy and material flows, and emissions associ- ated with biogas production and utilization. Analyses were based on literature data and state-of-the art technologies for biogas systems mainly relevant to conditions in Germany, and supple- mented with the Ecoinvent v2.1 database available with SimaPro 7.2 (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The basic study assumptions were drawn

from a linked study that carried out life-cycle energy audits of biogas production systems (Pöschl et al., 2010). A detailed account of the LCI results are presented in Poeschl et al. in press, upon which the unit processes offering the greatest opportunities for emission to air reduction, and therefore potential for step-change in envi- ronmental impact mitigation with biogas fuel were identified.

2.2.1.3. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA). The LCIA phase enabled the calculation of impact categories (Pennington et al., 2004). The LCIA was carried out on the basis of the inventory analysis data generated for the unit processes (Poeschl et al., in press), which were classified according to their potential impact on the envi- ronment. The full LCIA includes emissions to air, soil and water and was used to improve the understanding of the results of the LCI phase, by funneling the flows to retain only the dominant envi- ronmental factors (ISO, 2006a).

The ReCiPe Midpoint (H) and ReCiPe Endpoint (H/A) LCIA methodologies (Version 1.04, March 2010) in SimaPro 7.2 software (Goedkopp et al., 2009) were used to evaluate the life-cycle impacts. The ReCiPe methodology aligns two families of methods for LCIA: the midpoint-oriented CML 2002 method (CML, 2001) and the endpoint-oriented Eco-indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001). The indicators at two levels are determined, as: (i) eighteen midpoint indicators, and (ii) three endpoint indicators. The midpoint methodology was used to understand the complexity of impacts to emissions to air, water and soil to the environment for the different biogas production and utilization scenarios investi- gated (see Section 3 later). The endpoint methodology was used to analyze actual damages on the endpoints. For the purpose of sustainability assessment and comparability of LCIA results or sensitivity analysis of biogas production and utilization pathways analyzed (see Section 4 later) the LCIA results were presented as

single-score. The discussion in Sections 3 and 4 isolated the most significant outcomes, thereby identifying the potential 'hot-spots' where further mitigation of environmental impacts could be ach- ieved. The impact assessment parameters, as well as the normali- zation and weighting factors are summarized in Appendix - Table A5.

Fig. 2 illustrates how outcomes of the LCI were translated to life- cycle impacts in this study. The LCI data were classified, i.e., assigned to different impact categories, e.g., climate change, ozone depletion, etc. (Lunghi et al., 2004; ISO, 2006b; Pennington et al., 2004). Characterization was necessary, since their actual impacts may be dependent upon, say, the concentrations of contaminants in the environment exceeding set thresholds, due to temporal and special variability of the multiple sources. After characterization and damage assessment, the values were normalized by dividing all impact categories by the estimated annual impact of a European citizen. The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability impact categories in the ratios of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points. This LCA methodology has been successfully applied for analysis of geothermal energy systems (Saner et al., 2010), Municipal Solid Waste management scenarios in China (Hong et al., 2010), and assessment of biofuels (Suer and Andersson-Sköld, 2011; Banerjee and Tierney, 2011).

2.2.2. Evaluation of life-cycle energy conversion efficiency Measuring sustainability of the energy supply systems is a major issue as well as a driving force for the development of sustainable systems, and assessment of energy efficiency in respect to the consumption of primary energy to the energy output is a key

(in press) presented the detailed LCI of the scenarios considered in the study, while the results on characterized impact categories and weighted single-score LCA results are presented in Section 3 and 4, respectively.

technical consideration (Wang et al., 2009). Compatible savings in primary energy consumption can be reached by conversion tech- nologies using renewable energy sources, or reduction of final energy consumptions, under the same operating conditions. Biogas production and utilization pathways were evaluated on the basis of Primary Energy Input to Output (PEIO) ratio. Input was the sum of energy used in; crop cultivation and feedstock pre-treatment, feedstock collection and transport, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization and digestate processing and handling. Energy output was the sum of potential energy conversion from the biogas yield from the respective feedstock. A detailed description of the anal- yses is presented by Pöschl et al. 2010.

Evaluation of life-cycle energy conversion efficiency Measuring sustainability of the energy supply systems is a major issue as well as a driving force for the development of sustainable systems, and assessment of energy efficiency in respect to the consumption of primary energy to the energy output is a key

Compatible savings in primary energy consumption can be reached by conversion tech- nologies using renewable energy sources, or reduction of final energy consumptions, under the same operating conditions. Biogas production and utilization pathways were evaluated on the basis of Primary Energy Input to Output (PEIO) ratio. Input was the sum of energy used in; crop cultivation and feedstock pre-treatment, feedstock collection and transport, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization and digestate processing and handling. 8: Evaluation criteria and methods that can reliably assess the potential environmental sustainability of renewable energy system deployment is prerequisite for selection of the optimal alternative and informing decision-makers of the integrated performances of the alternatives, including the monitoring of impact on the social environment. A sustainable energy system balances energy production and consumption, with minimal negative impact on the environment, coupled with inherent opportunities for imple- mentation of social and economic activities (Hofman and Li, 2009). Wang et al. (2009) outlined the appropriate multi-criteria decision- making procedure for sustainable energy systems to include: (i) technology (primary energy ratio, etc.); (ii) economy (investment costs, etc.); (iii) environment (CO2 emissions, etc.), and; (iv) society (job creation, etc.). In this study the potential environmental impacts of biogas production and utilization pathways (Section 2.2.1) were evaluated vis-à-vis earlier results by the authors on technology assessment of identical case scenarios (Pöschl et al., 2010) (Section 2.2.2), and economic and social considerations

underpinning sustainable biogas production and utilization (Poeschl et al., 2010).

The evaluation of the environmental impacts, hence, sustain- ability of a technological option requires an LCA of a product or system, i.e., from raw material acquisition to final disposal (from cradle to grave), usually in four successive stages of analyses, including: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI); (iii) Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA), and; (iv) interpre- tation of the results (Contreras et al., 2009). Outcomes of the detailed LCI analysis for the systems under consideration in this study have been reported in Poeschl et al. in press. This paper therefore focused on the LCIA and interpretation of the results following the LCA study principles as specified in the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The analyses were done by attributional LCA, to understand the environmental impacts directly associated with the life-cycle (EC, 2010), using SimaPro LCA software (Pré Consultants, 2009).

2.2.1.1. Goal and scope definition. The main goal was to conduct an LCIA to enable a comparison of environmental and health impacts (viz., damage to human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability) of different biogas production and utilization path- ways, against the declared base scenarios. The LCIA was to provide the basis for identification of pathway-dependent environmental burdens, for possible improvement of associated technologies and processes in order to enhance the environmental sustainability of biogas as a renewable energy resource. In such context, the results inform about environmental compatible technologies for biogas production and utilization. The analyses covered the environmental and public health impacts associated with the entire biogas production chain outlined in Fig. 1. Sensitivity analysis was done to quantify the potential impacts of: (i) feedstock and digestate transportation range, (ii) variation in biogas yield in MSW, and; (iii)
electricity supply source in biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics.

Biogas plants currently rely on alternative feedstock from food processing and MSW streams, due to their easy availability, and also high cost of alternative energy crop feedstock. High feedstock demand has led to reduction in gate fees, therefore biogas plants are more attractive for management of organic waste (Poeschl et al., 2010). The main process in biogas production system is the AD of organic material for biogas production, which, after cleaning and secondary processing, could be used to generate electricity and heat, and in direct applications as substitute for natural gas or as transportation fuel. The functional unit applied in the assessment was the treatment of 1 tonne of organic material (feedstock) in small and large-scale biogas plants. The biogas generated was considered as main product, which contributes to the total envi- ronmental load. The by-products were included by system expan- sion (Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011), hence, digestate and energy derived from biogas, were considered to substitute other products in the market. The system boundary (Fig. 1) considered the production of chemical fertilizers, electricity generation with Ger- many's fuel mix, heat generation using natural gas, as well as imports of natural gas and transportation fuel. Their equivalent environmental loads were subtracted from the scenario's total environmental impacts.

2.2.1.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI). The LCI involved the compilation and quantification of inputs and outputs, for biogas production and utilization throughout the product life-cycle (ISO, 2006a). A number of different data sources were used to generate a system- atic inventory of energy and material flows, and emissions associ- ated with biogas production and utilization. Analyses were based on literature data and state-of-the art technologies for biogas systems mainly relevant to conditions in Germany, and supple- mented with the Ecoinvent v2.1 database available with SimaPro 7.2 (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The basic study assumptions were drawn

from a linked study that carried out life-cycle energy audits of biogas production systems (Pöschl et al., 2010). A detailed account of the LCI results are presented in Poeschl et al. in press, upon which the unit processes offering the greatest opportunities for emission to air reduction, and therefore potential for step-change in envi- ronmental impact mitigation with biogas fuel were identified.

2.2.1.3. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA). The LCIA phase enabled the calculation of impact categories (Pennington et al., 2004). The LCIA was carried out on the basis of the inventory analysis data generated for the unit processes (Poeschl et al., in press), which were classified according to their potential impact on the envi- ronment. The full LCIA includes emissions to air, soil and water and was used to improve the understanding of the results of the LCI phase, by funneling the flows to retain only the dominant envi- ronmental factors (ISO, 2006a).

The ReCiPe Midpoint (H) and ReCiPe Endpoint (H/A) LCIA methodologies (Version 1.04, March 2010) in SimaPro 7.2 software (Goedkopp et al., 2009) were used to evaluate the life-cycle impacts. The ReCiPe methodology aligns two families of methods for LCIA: the midpoint-oriented CML 2002 method (CML, 2001) and the endpoint-oriented Eco-indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001). The indicators at two levels are determined, as: (i) eighteen midpoint indicators, and (ii) three endpoint indicators. The midpoint methodology was used to understand the complexity of impacts to emissions to air, water and soil to the environment for the different biogas production and utilization scenarios investi- gated (see Section 3 later). The endpoint methodology was used to analyze actual damages on the endpoints. For the purpose of sustainability assessment and comparability of LCIA results or sensitivity analysis of biogas production and utilization pathways analyzed (see Section 4 later) the LCIA results were presented as

single-score. The discussion in Sections 3 and 4 isolated the most significant outcomes, thereby identifying the potential 'hot-spots' where further mitigation of environmental impacts could be ach- ieved. The impact assessment parameters, as well as the normali- zation and weighting factors are summarized in Appendix - Table A5.

Fig. 2 illustrates how outcomes of the LCI were translated to life- cycle impacts in this study. The LCI data were classified, i.e., assigned to different impact categories, e.g., climate change, ozone depletion, etc. (Lunghi et al., 2004; ISO, 2006b; Pennington et al., 2004). Characterization was necessary, since their actual impacts may be dependent upon, say, the concentrations of contaminants in the environment exceeding set thresholds, due to temporal and special variability of the multiple sources. After characterization and damage assessment, the values were normalized by dividing all impact categories by the estimated annual impact of a European citizen. The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability impact categories in the ratios of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points. This LCA methodology has been successfully applied for analysis of geothermal energy systems (Saner et al., 2010), Municipal Solid Waste management scenarios in China (Hong et al., 2010), and assessment of biofuels (Suer and Andersson-Sköld, 2011; Banerjee and Tierney, 2011).

electricity supply source in biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics.

Biogas plants currently rely on alternative feedstock from food processing and MSW streams, due to their easy availability, and also high cost of alternative energy crop feedstock. High feedstock demand has led to reduction in gate fees, therefore biogas plants are more attractive for management of organic waste (Poeschl et al., 2010). The main process in biogas production system is the AD of organic material for biogas production, which, after cleaning and secondary processing, could be used to generate electricity and heat, and in direct applications as substitute for natural gas or as transportation fuel. The functional unit applied in the assessment was the treatment of 1 tonne of organic material (feedstock) in small and large-scale biogas plants. The biogas generated was considered as main product, which contributes to the total envi- ronmental load. The by-products were included by system expan- sion (Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011), hence, digestate and energy derived from biogas, were considered to substitute other products in the market. The system boundary (Fig. 1) considered the production of chemical fertilizers, electricity generation with Ger- many's fuel mix, heat generation using natural gas, as well as imports of natural gas and transportation fuel. Their equivalent environmental loads were subtracted from the scenario's total environmental impacts.

2.2.1.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI). The LCI involved the compilation and quantification of inputs and outputs, for biogas production and utilization throughout the product life-cycle (ISO, 2006a). A number of different data sources were used to generate a system- atic inventory of energy and material flows, and emissions associ- ated with biogas production and utilization. Analyses were based on literature data and state-of-the art technologies for biogas systems mainly relevant to conditions in Germany, and supple- mented with the Ecoinvent v2.1 database available with SimaPro 7.2 (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The basic study assumptions were drawn

from a linked study that carried out life-cycle energy audits of biogas production systems (Pöschl et al., 2010). A detailed account of the LCI results are presented in Poeschl et al. in press, upon which the unit processes offering the greatest opportunities for emission to air reduction, and therefore potential for step-change in envi- ronmental impact mitigation with biogas fuel were identified.

2.2.1.3. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA). The LCIA phase enabled the calculation of impact categories (Pennington et al., 2004). The LCIA was carried out on the basis of the inventory analysis data generated for the unit processes (Poeschl et al., in press), which were classified according to their potential impact on the envi- ronment. The full LCIA includes emissions to air, soil and water and was used to improve the understanding of the results of the LCI phase, by funneling the flows to retain only the dominant envi- ronmental factors (ISO, 2006a).

The ReCiPe Midpoint (H) and ReCiPe Endpoint (H/A) LCIA methodologies (Version 1.04, March 2010) in SimaPro 7.2 software (Goedkopp et al., 2009) were used to evaluate the life-cycle impacts. The ReCiPe methodology aligns two families of methods for LCIA: the midpoint-oriented CML 2002 method (CML, 2001) and the endpoint-oriented Eco-indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001). The indicators at two levels are determined, as: (i) eighteen midpoint indicators, and (ii) three endpoint indicators. The midpoint methodology was used to understand the complexity of impacts to emissions to air, water and soil to the environment for the different biogas production and utilization scenarios investi- gated (see Section 3 later). The endpoint methodology was used to analyze actual damages on the endpoints. For the purpose of sustainability assessment and comparability of LCIA results or sensitivity analysis of biogas production and utilization pathways analyzed (see Section 4 later) the LCIA results were presented as

single-score. The discussion in Sections 3 and 4 isolated the most significant outcomes, thereby identifying the potential 'hot-spots' where further mitigation of environmental impacts could be ach- ieved. The impact assessment parameters, as well as the normali- zation and weighting factors are summarized in Appendix - Table A5.

Fig. 2 illustrates how outcomes of the LCI were translated to life- cycle impacts in this study. The LCI data were classified, i.e., assigned to different impact categories, e.g., climate change, ozone depletion, etc. (Lunghi et al., 2004; ISO, 2006b; Pennington et al., 2004). Characterization was necessary, since their actual impacts may be dependent upon, say, the concentrations of contaminants in the environment exceeding set thresholds, due to temporal and special variability of the multiple sources. After characterization and damage assessment, the values were normalized by dividing all impact categories by the estimated annual impact of a European citizen. The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability impact categories in the ratios of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points. This LCA methodology has been successfully applied for analysis of geothermal energy systems (Saner et al., 2010), Municipal Solid Waste management scenarios in China (Hong et al., 2010), and assessment of biofuels (Suer and Andersson-Sköld, 2011; Banerjee and Tierney, 2011).

2.2.2. Evaluation of life-cycle energy conversion efficiency Measuring sustainability of the energy supply systems is a major issue as well as a driving force for the development of sustainable systems, and assessment of energy efficiency in respect to the consumption of primary energy to the energy output is a key

(in press) presented the detailed LCI of the scenarios considered in the study, while the results on characterized impact categories and weighted single-score LCA results are presented in Section 3 and 4, respectively.

technical consideration (Wang et al., 2009). Compatible savings in primary energy consumption can be reached by conversion tech- nologies using renewable energy sources, or reduction of final energy consumptions, under the same operating conditions. Biogas production and utilization pathways were evaluated on the basis of Primary Energy Input to Output (PEIO) ratio. Input was the sum of energy used in; crop cultivation and feedstock pre-treatment, feedstock collection and transport, biogas plant operation, biogas utilization and digestate processing and handling. Energy output was the sum of potential energy conversion from the biogas yield from the respective feedstock. A detailed description of the anal- yses is presented by Pöschl et al. 2010.
12: Evaluation criteria and methods that can reliably assess the potential environmental sustainability of renewable energy system deployment is prerequisite for selection of the optimal alternative and informing decision-makers of the integrated performances of the alternatives, including the monitoring of impact on the social environment. A sustainable energy system balances energy production and consumption, with minimal negative impact on the environment, coupled with inherent opportunities for imple- mentation of social and economic activities (Hofman and Li, 2009). Wang et al. (2009) outlined the appropriate multi-criteria decision- making procedure for sustainable energy systems to include: (i) technology (primary energy ratio, etc.); (ii) economy (investment costs, etc.); (iii) environment (CO2 emissions, etc.), and; (iv) society (job creation, etc.). In this study the potential environmental impacts of biogas production and utilization pathways (Section 2.2.1) were evaluated vis-à-vis earlier results by the authors on technology assessment of identical case scenarios (Pöschl et al., 2010) (Section 2.2.2), and economic and social considerations

underpinning sustainable biogas production and utilization (Poeschl et al., 2010).

The evaluation of the environmental impacts, hence, sustain- ability of a technological option requires an LCA of a product or system, i.e., from raw material acquisition to final disposal (from cradle to grave), usually in four successive stages of analyses, including: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI); (iii) Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA), and; (iv) interpre- tation of the results (Contreras et al., 2009). Outcomes of the detailed LCI analysis for the systems under consideration in this study have been reported in Poeschl et al. in press. This paper therefore focused on the LCIA and interpretation of the results following the LCA study principles as specified in the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The analyses were done by attributional LCA, to understand the environmental impacts directly associated with the life-cycle (EC, 2010), using SimaPro LCA software (Pré Consultants, 2009).

The impact assessment parameters, as well as the normali- zation and weighting factors are summarized in Appendix - Table A5.

Fig. 2 illustrates how outcomes of the LCI were translated to life- cycle impacts in this study. The LCI data were classified, i.e., assigned to different impact categories, e.g., climate change, ozone depletion, etc. (Lunghi et al., 2004; ISO, 2006b; Pennington et al., 2004). Characterization was necessary, since their actual impacts may be dependent upon, say, the concentrations of contaminants in the environment exceeding set thresholds, due to temporal and special variability of the multiple sources. After characterization and damage assessment, the values were normalized by dividing all impact categories by the estimated annual impact of a European citizen. The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability impact categories in the ratios of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points. This LCA methodology has been successfully applied for analysis of geothermal energy systems (Saner et al., 2010), Municipal Solid Waste management scenarios in China (Hong et al., 2010), and assessment of biofuels (Suer and Andersson-Sköld, 2011; Banerjee and Tierney, 2011).

Compatible savings in primary energy consumption can be reached by conversion tech- nologies using renewable energy sources, or reduction of final energy consumptions, under the same operating conditions. Biogas production and utilization pathways were evaluated on the basis of Primary Energy Input to Output (PEIO) ratio. 15: Evaluation criteria and methods that can reliably assess the potential environmental sustainability of renewable energy system deployment is prerequisite for selection of the optimal alternative and informing decision-makers of the integrated performances of the alternatives, including the monitoring of impact on the social environment. A sustainable energy system balances energy production and consumption, with minimal negative impact on the environment, coupled with inherent opportunities for imple- mentation of social and economic activities (Hofman and Li, 2009). Wang et al. (2009) outlined the appropriate multi-criteria decision- making procedure for sustainable energy systems to include: (i) technology (primary energy ratio, etc.); (ii) economy (investment costs, etc.); (iii) environment (CO2 emissions, etc.), and; (iv) society (job creation, etc.). In this study the potential environmental impacts of biogas production and utilization pathways (Section 2.2.1) were evaluated vis-à-vis earlier results by the authors on technology assessment of identical case scenarios (Pöschl et al., 2010) (Section 2.2.2), and economic and social considerations

underpinning sustainable biogas production and utilization (Poeschl et al., 2010).

The evaluation of the environmental impacts, hence, sustain- ability of a technological option requires an LCA of a product or system, i.e., from raw material acquisition to final disposal (from cradle to grave), usually in four successive stages of analyses, including: (i) goal and scope definition; (ii) Life Cycle Inventory (LCI); (iii) Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA), and; (iv) interpre- tation of the results (Contreras et al., 2009). Outcomes of the detailed LCI analysis for the systems under consideration in this study have been reported in Poeschl et al. in press. This paper therefore focused on the LCIA and interpretation of the results following the LCA study principles as specified in the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The analyses were done by attributional LCA, to understand the environmental impacts directly associated with the life-cycle (EC, 2010), using SimaPro LCA software (Pré Consultants, 2009).

2.2.1.1. Goal and scope definition. The main goal was to conduct an LCIA to enable a comparison of environmental and health impacts (viz., damage to human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability) of different biogas production and utilization path- ways, against the declared base scenarios. The LCIA was to provide the basis for identification of pathway-dependent environmental burdens, for possible improvement of associated technologies and processes in order to enhance the environmental sustainability of biogas as a renewable energy resource. In such context, the results inform about environmental compatible technologies for biogas production and utilization. The analyses covered the environmental and public health impacts associated with the entire biogas production chain outlined in Fig. 1. Sensitivity analysis was done to quantify the potential impacts of: (i) feedstock and digestate transportation range, (ii) variation in biogas yield in MSW, and; (iii)
electricity supply source in biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics.

Biogas plants currently rely on alternative feedstock from food processing and MSW streams, due to their easy availability, and also high cost of alternative energy crop feedstock. High feedstock demand has led to reduction in gate fees, therefore biogas plants are more attractive for management of organic waste (Poeschl et al., 2010). The main process in biogas production system is the AD of organic material for biogas production, which, after cleaning and secondary processing, could be used to generate electricity and heat, and in direct applications as substitute for natural gas or as transportation fuel. The functional unit applied in the assessment was the treatment of 1 tonne of organic material (feedstock) in small and large-scale biogas plants. The biogas generated was considered as main product, which contributes to the total envi- ronmental load. The by-products were included by system expan- sion (Börjesson and Tufvesson, 2011), hence, digestate and energy derived from biogas, were considered to substitute other products in the market. The system boundary (Fig. 1) considered the production of chemical fertilizers, electricity generation with Ger- many's fuel mix, heat generation using natural gas, as well as imports of natural gas and transportation fuel. Their equivalent environmental loads were subtracted from the scenario's total environmental impacts.

2.2.1.2. Life cycle inventory (LCI). The LCI involved the compilation and quantification of inputs and outputs, for biogas production and utilization throughout the product life-cycle (ISO, 2006a). A number of different data sources were used to generate a system- atic inventory of energy and material flows, and emissions associ- ated with biogas production and utilization. Analyses were based on literature data and state-of-the art technologies for biogas systems mainly relevant to conditions in Germany, and supple- mented with the Ecoinvent v2.1 database available with SimaPro 7.2 (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The basic study assumptions were drawn

from a linked study that carried out life-cycle energy audits of biogas production systems (Pöschl et al., 2010). A detailed account of the LCI results are presented in Poeschl et al. in press, upon which the unit processes offering the greatest opportunities for emission to air reduction, and therefore potential for step-change in envi- ronmental impact mitigation with biogas fuel were identified.

2.2.1.3. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA). The LCIA phase enabled the calculation of impact categories (Pennington et al., 2004). The LCIA was carried out on the basis of the inventory analysis data generated for the unit processes (Poeschl et al., in press), which were classified according to their potential impact on the envi- ronment. The full LCIA includes emissions to air, soil and water and was used to improve the understanding of the results of the LCI phase, by funneling the flows to retain only the dominant envi- ronmental factors (ISO, 2006a).

The ReCiPe Midpoint (H) and ReCiPe Endpoint (H/A) LCIA methodologies (Version 1.04, March 2010) in SimaPro 7.2 software (Goedkopp et al., 2009) were used to evaluate the life-cycle impacts. The ReCiPe methodology aligns two families of methods for LCIA: the midpoint-oriented CML 2002 method (CML, 2001) and the endpoint-oriented Eco-indicator 99 method (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001). The indicators at two levels are determined, as: (i) eighteen midpoint indicators, and (ii) three endpoint indicators. The midpoint methodology was used to understand the complexity of impacts to emissions to air, water and soil to the environment for the different biogas production and utilization scenarios investi- gated (see Section 3 later). The endpoint methodology was used to analyze actual damages on the endpoints. For the purpose of sustainability assessment and comparability of LCIA results or sensitivity analysis of biogas production and utilization pathways analyzed (see Section 4 later) the LCIA results were presented as

single-score. The discussion in Sections 3 and 4 isolated the most significant outcomes, thereby identifying the potential 'hot-spots' where further mitigation of environmental impacts could be ach- ieved. The impact assessment parameters, as well as the normali- zation and weighting factors are summarized in Appendix - Table A5.

Fig. 2 illustrates how outcomes of the LCI were translated to life- cycle impacts in this study. The LCI data were classified, i.e., assigned to different impact categories, e.g., climate change, ozone depletion, etc. (Lunghi et al., 2004; ISO, 2006b; Pennington et al., 2004). Characterization was necessary, since their actual impacts may be dependent upon, say, the concentrations of contaminants in the environment exceeding set thresholds, due to temporal and special variability of the multiple sources. After characterization and damage assessment, the values were normalized by dividing all impact categories by the estimated annual impact of a European citizen. The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability impact categories in the ratios of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points. This LCA methodology has been successfully applied for analysis of geothermal energy systems (Saner et al., 2010), Municipal Solid Waste management scenarios in China (Hong et al., 2010), and assessment of biofuels (Suer and Andersson-Sköld, 2011; Banerjee and Tierney, 2011).

18: <header>3. Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) results and interpretation </header>

In this section, life-cycle impacts arising from the considered variations in unit processes associated with feedstock type, biogas utilization, and digestate processing and handling are presented. LCIA midpoint results help to improve the understanding of the complexity of impacts to emissions to air, water and soil to impact categories. Positive values signify increased environmental impacts, whereas negative values signify reduced environmental impacts accrued from substitution of fossil fuels with biogas and chemical fertilizers with nutrients from the digestate (see Fig. 1). Indicated values are given per tonne of feedstock (see also Section 2.2.1.1).
19: The LCIA results for single feedstock digestion are presented in Table 1. The results show that there were significant variations in all

impact categories among the scenarios considered, which indicates that feedstock choice is important in management of life-cycle impacts in single feedstock digestion. For example, for climate change (CC) impact category, i.e., greenhouse effect, straw feed- stock could achieve up to ten times higher impact reduction compared to base scenario of cattle manure (-23.22 kg CO2-eq). The main attribute was the higher biogas yield for straw (293.2 m³ compared to cattle manure leading to corresponding substitution of fuel for electricity generation in Germany's production mix (Pöschl et al., 2010).

Table 1 also shows that utilization of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage in single feedstock digestion would incur comparatively higher environmental degradation, especially in relation to: the particulate matter formation (PMF) impact cate- gory associated with respiratory problems in humans due to several inorganic substances and dust (viz., NOx, SO2 and PM10); and freshwater ecotoxicity (FET) due to toxic substances that are emitted to air, water and soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009). This observation was mainly attributed to the higher fossil fuel consumption associated with mechanized tillage, sowing, weed control, fertilization, pest and pathogen control and harvesting (Ecoinvent, 2007a). Furthermore, impacts from agricultural land occupation (ALO), representing land requirements, calculated on the basis of the area transformed to produce 1 kg of product, and average restoration time, are comparatively higher for grass silage and whole wheat plant silage (Heijungs et al., 2003). Generally, cultivated feedstock had significantly higher ALO impact as would be expected. For instance, whole wheat plant silage (occupation factor of 1.144 m²a per kg), corn silage (occupation factor of 0.059 m²a per kg) and grass silage (occupation factor of 0.834 m²a per kg) (Ecoinvent, 2007b), required 715.90, 185.28 and 834.92 m²a per tonne of feedstock, respectively, against 1.03 m²a for base feedstock of cattle manure (Pré Consultants, 2009). Feedstock origin was shown to influence the impact categories of freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), and metal depletion (MD). The FE and ME impacts are associated with phosphorus concentration in freshwater and nitrogen enrichment in seawater, respectively, whereas MD represents the exploitation of mineral resources with finite availability (Heijungs et al., 2003). Single feedstock digestion of pomace incurred about 18% MD and 1.5% ME impacts of the values for whole wheat plant silage (19.60 kg Fe-eq; 7.25 kg N-eq). The higher values for whole wheat plant silage cultivation were attributed to fertilizer input (ME, FE) and more fuel intense feedstock handling, in addition to only the transportation requirements for pomace feedstock (Poeschl et al., in press).

The results also show that photochemical oxidant formation (POF) and PMF impacts associated with MSW were up to 3 times higher compared to residues from food industry (Table 1). POF impacts, also called ozone or smog formation, are mainly caused by exhaust emissions like carbon monoxide (CO), non-methane vola- tile organic compounds (NMVOC) and sulfur dioxide (SO2), and are associated with increased frequency and severity respiratory distress in humans (EEA, 2008). Detailed LCI analysis had shown that NOx and NMVOC emissions where 26 and 8 times higher, respectively, for MSW, compared to food residues (47.7 g NOx; 6.5 g NMVOC) (Poeschl et al., in press). The NOx and NMVOC emissions were mainly attributed to the primary energy inputs for collection, transportation, and pre-treatment of MSW feedstock (1032 MJPE t-1 (Pöschl et al., 2010).

Impact categories of terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET), marine eco- toxicity (MET) and freshwater ecotoxicity (FET) describe the impacts on the ecosystem due to toxic substances that are emitted to air, water and soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009). The data show that energy crops would incur higher ecotoxicity impacts than cattle manure, predominantly due to the chemical fertilizers and pesti- cide inputs to the cultivation processes (Table 1). Ozone depletion (OD) and natural land transformation (NLT) impacts were negli- gible for single feedstock digestion.

Feedstock origin was shown to influence the impact categories of freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), and metal depletion (MD). The FE and ME impacts are associated with phosphorus concentration in freshwater and nitrogen enrichment in seawater, respectively, whereas MD represents the exploitation of mineral resources with finite availability (Heijungs et al., 2003). Single feedstock digestion of pomace incurred about 18% MD and 1.5% ME impacts of the values for whole wheat plant silage (19.60 kg Fe-eq; 7.25 kg N-eq). The higher values for whole wheat plant silage cultivation were attributed to fertilizer input (ME, FE) and more fuel intense feedstock handling, in addition to only the transportation requirements for pomace feedstock (Poeschl et al., in press).

The results also show that photochemical oxidant formation (POF) and PMF impacts associated with MSW were up to 3 times higher compared to residues from food industry (Table 1). POF impacts, also called ozone or smog formation, are mainly caused by exhaust emissions like carbon monoxide (CO), non-methane vola- tile organic compounds (NMVOC) and sulfur dioxide (SO2), and are associated with increased frequency and severity respiratory distress in humans (EEA, 2008). Detailed LCI analysis had shown that NOx and NMVOC emissions where 26 and 8 times higher, respectively, for MSW, compared to food residues (47.7 g NOx; 6.5 g NMVOC) (Poeschl et al., in press). The NOx and NMVOC emissions were mainly attributed to the primary energy inputs for collection, transportation, and pre-treatment of MSW feedstock (1032 MJPE t-1 (Pöschl et al., 2010).

Ozone depletion (OD) and natural land transformation (NLT) impacts were negli- gible for single feedstock digestion.

29: In order to determine how co-digestion of multiple feedstocks influence the environmental and public health impacts compared to single feedstock digestion, LCIA for six co-digestion scenarios in designated small and large-scale biogas plants were analyzed (Table 2). Human toxicity (HT) impact category accounts for toxic accumulation of substances in the human food chain and toxicity effects of chemicals, like carcinogenic substances. From Table 2, it is shown that the range of variations in the impact category values were lower than for single feedstock digestion (Table 1). For example, the HT impact variation approximately halved from 53.84 kg 1.4-DB-eq between straw (min) and whole wheat plant silage (max) for single feedstock digestion (Table 1), to -13.58 (Base SS) to 8.93 kg 1.4-DB-eq (Base LS-1) (22.51 kg 1.4-DB-eq between min and max) for co-digestion of multiple feedstocks, which indi- cates more stable processes with environmental compatible performance.

Generally, co-digestion scenarios with high proportion of energy crops (Base SS-1 and Base LS-1) had relatively higher environmental impacts as would be expected (Table 2). For example, the data for small-scale biogas plants showed that POF impact value was nearly twice as high for Base SS-1 (0.63 kg NMVOC), compared to base case Base SS (0.34 kg NMVOC). Analysis of single feedstock scenarios already showed (Table 1), that POF impact potential was 60% and 125% higher for grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively, compared to corn silage (0.67 kg NMVOC); hence, corn silage feedstock was more envi- ronmentally compatible. Co-digesting cattle manure with 35% corn silage, 18% grass silage and 22% whole wheat plant silage (Base LS- 1) incurred 7 times higher POF impacts compared to digesting mainly MSW and food industry residues (Base LS). Results in Table 1 already showed that residues from food industry incurred lower POF impacts of between 0.15 (grease separator sludge) and 0.30 kg NMVOC (pomace), in contrast to energy crops with 0.67 (corn silage) up to 1.51 kg NMVOC (whole wheat plant silage).

With respect to potential impacts on CC, Base SS (-75.38 kg CO2-eq) and Base LS-2 (-119.77 kg CO2-eq) scenarios registered highest impact reduction for small and large-scale biogas systems analyzed, respectively. The CO2-eq unit denotes entire GHG

emissions, such as CH4, fossil, CH4, biogenic and N20 having up to 25, 22 and 298 times higher equivalent global warming potentials, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The detailed LCI analysis (Poeschl et al., in press), previously showed that Base LS-1 incurred 120 times higher N20 emissions in the feedstock supply chain alone, compared to Base LS-2 (2.3 g N20), which may explain the quadrupling of the CC impact reduction potential for Base LS-2.

Results in Table 2 also show that environmental impacts from FET, MET and ALO were marginal for co-digestion of mainly MSW and residues of agriculture and food industry (Base SS-2, Base LS, Base LS-2), compared to scenarios co-digesting energy crops (Base SS, Base SS-1 and Base LS-1). For example, Base SS-2 incurred only 3% of the ALO impact of Base SS (84.21 m²a). Furthermore, Base SS-2 reduced the FET impacts (-0.03 kg 1.4-DB-eq), compared to Base SS (0.34 kg 1.4-DB-eq). The difference arises from the fact that agricultural and food industry residues incur up to 130 times lower FET impacts (e.g., 0.02 kg 1.4-DB-eq for food residues) and may even enhance FET impacts (e.g., -0.07 kg 1.4-DB-eq for straw) in single feedstock digestion, compared to energy crop feedstocks (e.g., 2.57 kg 1.4-DB-eq for wheat plant silage) (Table 1).

There was a strong positive correlation between biogas yield and the potential reduction in fossil depletion (FD) impacts, an indicator of the finiteness of fossil fuel supply. For example, biogas yield from Base LS-1 was 135% higher compared to Base LS (66.3 m³ therefore, FD impacts reduced by approximately 70% in Base LS-1 compared to Base LS (-18.76 kg oil-eq) (Table 2) (Pöschl et al., 2010).
30: In order to determine how co-digestion of multiple feedstocks influence the environmental and public health impacts compared to single feedstock digestion, LCIA for six co-digestion scenarios in designated small and large-scale biogas plants were analyzed (Table 2). Human toxicity (HT) impact category accounts for toxic accumulation of substances in the human food chain and toxicity effects of chemicals, like carcinogenic substances. From Table 2, it is shown that the range of variations in the impact category values were lower than for single feedstock digestion (Table 1). For example, the HT impact variation approximately halved from 53.84 kg 1.4-DB-eq between straw (min) and whole wheat plant silage (max) for single feedstock digestion (Table 1), to -13.58 (Base SS) to 8.93 kg 1.4-DB-eq (Base LS-1) (22.51 kg 1.4-DB-eq between min and max) for co-digestion of multiple feedstocks, which indi- cates more stable processes with environmental compatible performance.

Generally, co-digestion scenarios with high proportion of energy crops (Base SS-1 and Base LS-1) had relatively higher environmental impacts as would be expected (Table 2). For example, the data for small-scale biogas plants showed that POF impact value was nearly twice as high for Base SS-1 (0.63 kg NMVOC), compared to base case Base SS (0.34 kg NMVOC). Analysis of single feedstock scenarios already showed (Table 1), that POF impact potential was 60% and 125% higher for grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively, compared to corn silage (0.67 kg NMVOC); hence, corn silage feedstock was more envi- ronmentally compatible. Co-digesting cattle manure with 35% corn silage, 18% grass silage and 22% whole wheat plant silage (Base LS- 1) incurred 7 times higher POF impacts compared to digesting mainly MSW and food industry residues (Base LS). Results in Table 1 already showed that residues from food industry incurred lower POF impacts of between 0.15 (grease separator sludge) and 0.30 kg NMVOC (pomace), in contrast to energy crops with 0.67 (corn silage) up to 1.51 kg NMVOC (whole wheat plant silage).

emissions, such as CH4, fossil, CH4, biogenic and N20 having up to 25, 22 and 298 times higher equivalent global warming potentials, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b). The detailed LCI analysis (Poeschl et al., in press), previously showed that Base LS-1 incurred 120 times higher N20 emissions in the feedstock supply chain alone, compared to Base LS-2 (2.3 g N20), which may explain the quadrupling of the CC impact reduction potential for Base LS-2.

Results in Table 2 also show that environmental impacts from FET, MET and ALO were marginal for co-digestion of mainly MSW and residues of agriculture and food industry (Base SS-2, Base LS, Base LS-2), compared to scenarios co-digesting energy crops (Base SS, Base SS-1 and Base LS-1). For example, Base SS-2 incurred only 3% of the ALO impact of Base SS (84.21 m²a). Furthermore, Base SS-2 reduced the FET impacts (-0.03 kg 1.4-DB-eq), compared to Base SS (0.34 kg 1.4-DB-eq). The difference arises from the fact that agricultural and food industry residues incur up to 130 times lower FET impacts (e.g., 0.02 kg 1.4-DB-eq for food residues) and may even enhance FET impacts (e.g., -0.07 kg 1.4-DB-eq for straw) in single feedstock digestion, compared to energy crop feedstocks (e.g., 2.57 kg 1.4-DB-eq for wheat plant silage) (Table 1).

There was a strong positive correlation between biogas yield and the potential reduction in fossil depletion (FD) impacts, an indicator of the finiteness of fossil fuel supply. For example, biogas yield from Base LS-1 was 135% higher compared to Base LS (66.3 m³ therefore, FD impacts reduced by approximately 70% in Base LS-1 compared to Base LS (-18.76 kg oil-eq) (Table 2) (Pöschl et al., 2010).
34: In order to determine how co-digestion of multiple feedstocks influence the environmental and public health impacts compared to single feedstock digestion, LCIA for six co-digestion scenarios in designated small and large-scale biogas plants were analyzed (Table 2). Human toxicity (HT) impact category accounts for toxic accumulation of substances in the human food chain and toxicity effects of chemicals, like carcinogenic substances. From Table 2, it is shown that the range of variations in the impact category values were lower than for single feedstock digestion (Table 1). For example, the HT impact variation approximately halved from 53.84 kg 1.4-DB-eq between straw (min) and whole wheat plant silage (max) for single feedstock digestion (Table 1), to -13.58 (Base SS) to 8.93 kg 1.4-DB-eq (Base LS-1) (22.51 kg 1.4-DB-eq between min and max) for co-digestion of multiple feedstocks, which indi- cates more stable processes with environmental compatible performance.

Generally, co-digestion scenarios with high proportion of energy crops (Base SS-1 and Base LS-1) had relatively higher environmental impacts as would be expected (Table 2). For example, the data for small-scale biogas plants showed that POF impact value was nearly twice as high for Base SS-1 (0.63 kg NMVOC), compared to base case Base SS (0.34 kg NMVOC). Analysis of single feedstock scenarios already showed (Table 1), that POF impact potential was 60% and 125% higher for grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively, compared to corn silage (0.67 kg NMVOC); hence, corn silage feedstock was more envi- ronmentally compatible. Co-digesting cattle manure with 35% corn silage, 18% grass silage and 22% whole wheat plant silage (Base LS- 1) incurred 7 times higher POF impacts compared to digesting mainly MSW and food industry residues (Base LS). Results in Table 1 already showed that residues from food industry incurred lower POF impacts of between 0.15 (grease separator sludge) and 0.30 kg NMVOC (pomace), in contrast to energy crops with 0.67 (corn silage) up to 1.51 kg NMVOC (whole wheat plant silage).

37: The characterized LCIA results for the range of variations in biogas utilization process analyzed are shown in Table 3. The energy generated from biogas (viz., electricity, heat and bio- methane) substituted equivalent energy value from fossil fuels (Section 2.2.1.1). Reduced impacts were subtractive in the respec- tive biogas utilization pathways.

In comparing the small-scale biogas utilization scenarios deploying the same CHP unit for electricity generation (e.g., Base SS versus Base SS-b), the reduction of potential CC-related impacts were estimated to be more than doubled in case of Base SS- b (Table 3) compared to Base SS (-75.38 kg CO2-eq; Table 2). The underlying reason is that, in Base SS-b CHP generation was coupled to external heat utilization and cooling, hence, a higher combined efficiency of electricity and thermal energy conversion was realized (Nel = 33%, Nth = 50%, Nel (tri-generation) = 70% waste heat to cooling energy). Therefore higher substitution of electricity and heat generated from fossil fuels could be achieved, compared to the generation of electricity alone (Base SS, Nel = 33%) (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The PMF and TA (which describes the change in optimum of acidity of soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009)) follow a similar trend for scenarios analyzed (Table 3). For example, using Stirling engine for electricity generation coupled with external heat utilization (Base SS-d), reduced PMF and TA impacts by approximately 20%, compared to generation of electricity alone with the CHP unit (Base SS; 0.11 kg PM10-eq, 0.48 kg SO2-eq). On the other hand, coupling the CHP generation with Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) process (Base SS-c) PMF and TA impacts increased by approximately 40% compared to Base SS (Table 2). The explanation for this observation was that both impact categories are highly influenced by the system efficiency and the conversion technology. For example, Base SS-d benefited from higher combined electricity and thermal effi- ciency (Nel = 24%, Nth = 72%) compared to the Base SS efficiency (Nel = 33%), therefore higher fossil fuel substitution (Pöschl et al., 2010). The increased PMF and TA impacts in Base SS-c were due to additional inputs to the production and distribution of ORC infrastructure for use with the CHP generation (Frischknecht et al., 2007).

Data in Table 3 show, that scenarios that purify and upgrade biogas to biomethane (Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U), Base LS-C(U)) increased the CC impacts, in contrast to reductions in all other small and large-scale biogas utilization scenarios. CO2-eq reduction in CHP generation (Base LS, Table 2). This disparity for Base LS-A(U) could be attributed to the use of natural gas based heating to meet internal heat requirements, and the potential CH4 leakage associated with the upgrading and purifying process, which was assumed to be 3% (Pöschl et al., 2010). As CH4 has up to 25 times higher global warming potential of CO2, the CH4 leakage and CC impacts caused by natural gas based heating, easily offset the CC benefits from substituting natural gas with biomethane.

Results in Table 3 also show that all the variations in biogas utilization pathways in small and large-scale biogas plants, except Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U) and Base LS-C(U) which included the purification and upgrading of biogas to biomethane, reduced ionizing radiation impacts (IR). IR impacts are caused by the release of radioactive materials into the environment, especially in nuclear fuel cycle and in coal powered plants (Goedkopp et al., 2009). For example, Base LS-A(U) caused 14.81 kg per tonne of feed- stock mixture, in contrast to reduction of IR impacts for Base LS (-30.27 kg U23-eq) (Table 2). The disparity could be explained by the difference in fossil fuel substitution; electricity generated in Base LS was used to substitute electricity from Germany's fuel mix, which constitutes approximately 25% nuclear power and 43.5% of hard coal and lignite (Ecoinvent, 2007b). For Base LS-A(U) bio- methane derived from biogas production was used to replace natural gas, delivered by long-distance pipeline. Electricity produced by nuclear energy causes 80 and 1540 times higher IR impacts per MJ from lignite and natural gas, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b).

A significant observation in the large-scale biogas utilization scenarios was that the reductions in FD were duplicated for Base LS-D(U), compared to the -18.76 kg oil-eq for Base LS (Table 2). This could mainly be attributed to the high system efficiency for fuel cell technology with external heat utilization (Nel = 40%, Nth = 50%) (Pöschl et al., 2010). Data in Table 3 also show, that MD impact category increased by a factor of 4 in case of fuel cell technology application (Base LS-D(U)) compared to CHP utilization in Base LS (4.92 kg Fe-eq) (Table 2). The possible reason for the increase was the mineral resources intensive production of fuel cells, reflected in Ecoinvent v2.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2007b).
38: The characterized LCIA results for the range of variations in biogas utilization process analyzed are shown in Table 3. The energy generated from biogas (viz., electricity, heat and bio- methane) substituted equivalent energy value from fossil fuels (Section 2.2.1.1). Reduced impacts were subtractive in the respec- tive biogas utilization pathways.

In comparing the small-scale biogas utilization scenarios deploying the same CHP unit for electricity generation (e.g., Base SS versus Base SS-b), the reduction of potential CC-related impacts were estimated to be more than doubled in case of Base SS- b (Table 3) compared to Base SS (-75.38 kg CO2-eq; Table 2). The underlying reason is that, in Base SS-b CHP generation was coupled to external heat utilization and cooling, hence, a higher combined efficiency of electricity and thermal energy conversion was realized (Nel = 33%, Nth = 50%, Nel (tri-generation) = 70% waste heat to cooling energy). Therefore higher substitution of electricity and heat generated from fossil fuels could be achieved, compared to the generation of electricity alone (Base SS, Nel = 33%) (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The PMF and TA (which describes the change in optimum of acidity of soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009)) follow a similar trend for scenarios analyzed (Table 3). For example, using Stirling engine for electricity generation coupled with external heat utilization (Base SS-d), reduced PMF and TA impacts by approximately 20%, compared to generation of electricity alone with the CHP unit (Base SS; 0.11 kg PM10-eq, 0.48 kg SO2-eq). On the other hand, coupling the CHP generation with Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) process (Base SS-c) PMF and TA impacts increased by approximately 40% compared to Base SS (Table 2). The explanation for this observation was that both impact categories are highly influenced by the system efficiency and the conversion technology. For example, Base SS-d benefited from higher combined electricity and thermal effi- ciency (Nel = 24%, Nth = 72%) compared to the Base SS efficiency (Nel = 33%), therefore higher fossil fuel substitution (Pöschl et al., 2010). The increased PMF and TA impacts in Base SS-c were due to additional inputs to the production and distribution of ORC infrastructure for use with the CHP generation (Frischknecht et al., 2007).

Data in Table 3 show, that scenarios that purify and upgrade biogas to biomethane (Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U), Base LS-C(U)) increased the CC impacts, in contrast to reductions in all other small and large-scale biogas utilization scenarios. For Base LS-A(U) bio- methane derived from biogas production was used to replace natural gas, delivered by long-distance pipeline. Electricity produced by nuclear energy causes 80 and 1540 times higher IR impacts per MJ from lignite and natural gas, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b).

A significant observation in the large-scale biogas utilization scenarios was that the reductions in FD were duplicated for Base LS-D(U), compared to the -18.76 kg oil-eq for Base LS (Table 2). This could mainly be attributed to the high system efficiency for fuel cell technology with external heat utilization (Nel = 40%, Nth = 50%) (Pöschl et al., 2010). Data in Table 3 also show, that MD impact category increased by a factor of 4 in case of fuel cell technology application (Base LS-D(U)) compared to CHP utilization in Base LS (4.92 kg Fe-eq) (Table 2). The possible reason for the increase was the mineral resources intensive production of fuel cells, reflected in Ecoinvent v2.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2007b).

The energy generated from biogas (viz., electricity, heat and bio- methane) substituted equivalent energy value from fossil fuels (Section 2.2.1.1). Reduced impacts were subtractive in the respec- tive biogas utilization pathways.

In comparing the small-scale biogas utilization scenarios deploying the same CHP unit for electricity generation (e.g., Base SS versus Base SS-b), the reduction of potential CC-related impacts were estimated to be more than doubled in case of Base SS- b (Table 3) compared to Base SS (-75.38 kg CO2-eq; Table 2). The underlying reason is that, in Base SS-b CHP generation was coupled to external heat utilization and cooling, hence, a higher combined efficiency of electricity and thermal energy conversion was realized (Nel = 33%, Nth = 50%, Nel (tri-generation) = 70% waste heat to cooling energy). Therefore higher substitution of electricity and heat generated from fossil fuels could be achieved, compared to the generation of electricity alone (Base SS, Nel = 33%) (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The PMF and TA (which describes the change in optimum of acidity of soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009)) follow a similar trend for scenarios analyzed (Table 3). On the other hand, coupling the CHP generation with Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) process (Base SS-c) PMF and TA impacts increased by approximately 40% compared to Base SS (Table 2). The explanation for this observation was that both impact categories are highly influenced by the system efficiency and the conversion technology. For example, Base SS-d benefited from higher combined electricity and thermal effi- ciency (Nel = 24%, Nth = 72%) compared to the Base SS efficiency (Nel = 33%), therefore higher fossil fuel substitution (Pöschl et al., 2010). The increased PMF and TA impacts in Base SS-c were due to additional inputs to the production and distribution of ORC infrastructure for use with the CHP generation (Frischknecht et al., 2007).

CO2-eq reduction in CHP generation (Base LS, Table 2). This disparity for Base LS-A(U) could be attributed to the use of natural gas based heating to meet internal heat requirements, and the potential CH4 leakage associated with the upgrading and purifying process, which was assumed to be 3% (Pöschl et al., 2010). As CH4 has up to 25 times higher global warming potential of CO2, the CH4 leakage and CC impacts caused by natural gas based heating, easily offset the CC benefits from substituting natural gas with biomethane.

Results in Table 3 also show that all the variations in biogas utilization pathways in small and large-scale biogas plants, except Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U) and Base LS-C(U) which included the purification and upgrading of biogas to biomethane, reduced ionizing radiation impacts (IR). IR impacts are caused by the release of radioactive materials into the environment, especially in nuclear fuel cycle and in coal powered plants (Goedkopp et al., 2009). For example, Base LS-A(U) caused 14.81 kg per tonne of feed- stock mixture, in contrast to reduction of IR impacts for Base LS (-30.27 kg U23-eq) (Table 2). The disparity could be explained by the difference in fossil fuel substitution; electricity generated in Base LS was used to substitute electricity from Germany's fuel mix, which constitutes approximately 25% nuclear power and 43.5% of hard coal and lignite (Ecoinvent, 2007b). For Base LS-A(U) bio- methane derived from biogas production was used to replace natural gas, delivered by long-distance pipeline. Electricity produced by nuclear energy causes 80 and 1540 times higher IR impacts per MJ from lignite and natural gas, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b).

A significant observation in the large-scale biogas utilization scenarios was that the reductions in FD were duplicated for Base LS-D(U), compared to the -18.76 kg oil-eq for Base LS (Table 2). This could mainly be attributed to the high system efficiency for fuel cell technology with external heat utilization (Nel = 40%, Nth = 50%) (Pöschl et al., 2010). Data in Table 3 also show, that MD impact category increased by a factor of 4 in case of fuel cell technology application (Base LS-D(U)) compared to CHP utilization in Base LS (4.92 kg Fe-eq) (Table 2). The possible reason for the increase was the mineral resources intensive production of fuel cells, reflected in Ecoinvent v2.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2007b).
45: The characterized LCIA results for the range of variations in biogas utilization process analyzed are shown in Table 3. The energy generated from biogas (viz., electricity, heat and bio- methane) substituted equivalent energy value from fossil fuels (Section 2.2.1.1). Reduced impacts were subtractive in the respec- tive biogas utilization pathways.

In comparing the small-scale biogas utilization scenarios deploying the same CHP unit for electricity generation (e.g., Base SS versus Base SS-b), the reduction of potential CC-related impacts were estimated to be more than doubled in case of Base SS- b (Table 3) compared to Base SS (-75.38 kg CO2-eq; Table 2). The underlying reason is that, in Base SS-b CHP generation was coupled to external heat utilization and cooling, hence, a higher combined efficiency of electricity and thermal energy conversion was realized (Nel = 33%, Nth = 50%, Nel (tri-generation) = 70% waste heat to cooling energy). Therefore higher substitution of electricity and heat generated from fossil fuels could be achieved, compared to the generation of electricity alone (Base SS, Nel = 33%) (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The PMF and TA (which describes the change in optimum of acidity of soil (Goedkopp et al., 2009)) follow a similar trend for scenarios analyzed (Table 3). For example, using Stirling engine for electricity generation coupled with external heat utilization (Base SS-d), reduced PMF and TA impacts by approximately 20%, compared to generation of electricity alone with the CHP unit (Base SS; 0.11 kg PM10-eq, 0.48 kg SO2-eq). On the other hand, coupling the CHP generation with Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) process (Base SS-c) PMF and TA impacts increased by approximately 40% compared to Base SS (Table 2). The explanation for this observation was that both impact categories are highly influenced by the system efficiency and the conversion technology. For example, Base SS-d benefited from higher combined electricity and thermal effi- ciency (Nel = 24%, Nth = 72%) compared to the Base SS efficiency (Nel = 33%), therefore higher fossil fuel substitution (Pöschl et al., 2010). The increased PMF and TA impacts in Base SS-c were due to additional inputs to the production and distribution of ORC infrastructure for use with the CHP generation (Frischknecht et al., 2007).

Data in Table 3 show, that scenarios that purify and upgrade biogas to biomethane (Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U), Base LS-C(U)) increased the CC impacts, in contrast to reductions in all other small and large-scale biogas utilization scenarios.
