47: <header>3.3. Variations in digestate processing and handling </header>

The LCIA results for eight pathways with changes in the diges- tate processing and handling, in particular the impacts associated with digestate processing and handling (unit process IV) in Fig. 1, are shown in Table 4. Large-scale biogas plants often produce more digestate than could be disposed off in the vicinity of the biogas plant as fertilizer. The digestate contains active fertilizer ingredi- ents of triple superphosphate (P2O5), potassium sulfate (K2O) and ammonium nitrate (NH4-N) fertilizers; therefore it can substitute ingredients in chemical fertilizers.

Data in Table 4 show that reduction of CC impacts was only achieved in two scenarios, namely, Base LS-B(W)b and Base LS- C(W)b that realized -22.397 and -17.861 kg CO2-eq CC impact reductions per tonne of digested feedstock, respectively. Other scenarios analyzed registered CC impact increases of between 57.084 and 79.132 kg CO2-eq. The decreased impacts outlined above were attributed to recovery of up to 10% residual biogas from the covered digestate storage areas. The other scenarios deployed open storage areas, thereby allowing the residual biogas (approxi- mately 60% CH4 and 25 times higher CC impacts than CO2) to be emitted to the atmosphere (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The scenarios that included the spreading of solid fraction of digestate on arable land (Base LS-A(W), Base LS-C(W)a, Base LS- C(W)b) were the only variations that did not reduce ME impacts. Both scenarios incurred increased ME impacts of 0.005 kg N-eq, which can be attributed to the reduced availability of active fertil- izer ingredients in digestate arising from separation of the liquid fraction from the digestate. Reduced transportation requirements due to separated digestate marginally relieved ME impacts, compared to Base LS that considered the transportation of all the digestate (Poeschl et al., in press).

An important observation was that, the PMF, FE, FET, MET and MD impacts could be reduced for scenarios that included drying or composting of the solid fraction of digestate, compared to untreated digestate application (Base LS). For example, FE impacts could be reduced by 150% for Base LS-D(W), drying solid fraction, compared to Base LS (-0.006 kg P-eq, Table 4). This disparity was attributed to the active fertilizer ingredients in dried solid fraction of digestate

that could readily substitute chemical fertilizer production. The dried as well as composted solid fractions of the digestate contain high proportions of P2O5 (the active fertilizer ingredients in triple superphosphate fertilizers) (Poeschl et al., in press). Production and distribution of P2O5 fertilizer accounted for approximately 40 and 60 times higher impacts on FE, compared to active fertilizer ingredients of NH4-N and K20, respectively (Ecoinvent, 2007b). A clear distinction was also observed between drying and composting the solid fraction of digestate. For example, in Base LS-D(W) for which the solid fraction was dried, all impact categories could be improved compared to composting in Base LS-E(W) (Table 4).

In Germany, dried digestate has recently been tested in co-firing with coal or wood chips. If successful, the outcome could be particularly useful for secondary utilization of the digestate from MSW and food industry residues which are currently not recom- mended for land spreading. The International Biogas & Bioenergy Centre of Expertise (Thoss, 2008) and the Central Network for Agriculture-Resources-Marketing and Development (Arndt and Wagner, 2009), recorded suboptimal co-firing characteristics, therefore the technical and economic feasibility are still unclear and will require further research.

55: <header>4. Overall evaluation of biogas systems in multiple production and utilization pathways </header>

The overall impacts, hence, the potential sustainability of biogas production and utilization pathways was determined by com- pounded analyses of individual LCA results for biogas production, utilization, digestate treatment, and the life-cycle energy perfor- mance. The results in Fig. 3 to Fig. 6 show the single-score LCIA combining the three damage categories of human health (HH), ecosystems diversity (ED), and resources availability (RA) (Goedkopp et al., 2009). LCIA endpoint results make it easy to compare the results of different scenarios or sensitivity analysis. In all cases, positive values signify incurred environmental damage, whereas negative values signify reductions in cumulative damage. The cor- responding PEIO ratios are detailed in Pöschl et al. (2010). Indicated values are given per tonne of feedstock (see also Section 2.2.1.1).
56: <header>4.1. Single feedstock digestion scenarios </header>

The LCIA results in Fig. 3 identified agricultural waste and resi- dues (viz., cattle manure, straw), MSW, and food industry residues (viz., food residues, pomace, slaughterhouse waste, grease sepa- rator sludge) as most environmental compatible single feedstocks for biogas production. All were able to reduce the overall envi- ronmental impacts. This observation was mainly attributed to low emissions associated with feedstock handling (Poeschl et al., in press), and marginal impacts of agricultural residues on agricul- tural land occupation (ALO) (Section 3.1.1). It was shown, that the use of straw returned the most attractive environmental perfor- mance, improved by almost 830% compared to the base scenario of cattle manure feedstock.

Comparing the use of energy crop feedstocks for biogas production (viz., corn silage, grass silage, whole wheat plant silage), it was shown that corn silage feedstock was environ- mentally more attractive compared to grass silage or whole wheat plant silage (Fig. 3). For example, for corn silage the damages on ecosystems diversity (ED) were approximately 6 times lower, compared to grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. The ED was mainly influenced by ALO impacts, which strongly differ for the energy crops (Section 3.1.1). ALO impacts for corn silage were equivalent to 20% and 25% of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively (Table 1). This was reflected in overall envi- ronmental reductions for corn silage (-1.4 pt) compared to impacts for grass silage (45.6 pt) and whole wheat plant silage (41.4 pt) (Fig. 3).

Integrated assessment of life-cycle energy performance from individual feedstocks suggested straw and corn silage as the most energy efficient for biogas production (Pöschl et al., 2010). This was mainly ascribed to the higher energy density, which exceeded the primary energy inputs for feedstock supply logistics (Pöschl et al., 2010). Also, the MSW, food residues and slaughterhouse waste feedstocks required pre-treatment/sterilization and collection/ transportation regime prior to utilization as feedstocks in AD process, hence, achieved less attractive energy balances (Pöschl et al., 2010).

4.2. Co-digestion of multiple feedstock scenarios

Fig. 4 shows the weighted single-score LCIA results for the multiple feedstock co-digestion scenarios analyzed. It is shown that, for both small and large-scale co-digestion scenarios, co- digestion of mainly agricultural and food industry residues (Base SS-2; -5.2 pt) and MSW with residues of food industry (Base LS-2;
Large-scale biogas plants

-8.6 pt), respectively, were able to achieve highest cumulative damage impact reductions. In contrast, co-digestion of cattle manure with high proportion of energy crops exhibited the highest environmental impacts for both small (Base SS-1) and large-scale (Base LS-1) biogas plants. For small-scale plants, the environmental performance improved from an impact score of 6.1 points for co-digestion of cattle manure with 70% energy crops

The difference was mainly attributed to feedstock origins; e.g., feed- stocks of predominantly agricultural and food industry residue (Base SS-2) accounted for just 1% of impacts on agricultural land occupation (ALO) compared to the co-digestion of predominantly energy crop feedstock and also reduced the climate change impacts by up to 30% (Table 2). Therefore, to maximize the envi- ronmental impacts mitigation, higher proportion of agricultural residues and organic waste streams should be included in co- digestion scenarios.

The results also show, that co-digestion of corn silage with cattle manure (Base SS), will improve the overall environmental perfor- mance of single feedstock digestion of corn by over 100% (-2.9 pt) (Figs. 3 and 4). Also, the co-digestion of cattle manure with corn silage, grass silage and whole wheat plant silage significantly enhanced the environmental performance for small (Base SS-1) and large-scale (Base LS-1) biogas plants, respectively, compared to single feedstock digestion of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. With respect to energy balance (Pöschl et al., 2010), the results indicated that co-digestion of cattle manure with corn silage (Base SS), and MSW with food residues (Base LS) represented the most energy efficient co-digestion scenarios, for small and large- scale plants, respectively. Evidence from this study indicates that the environmental performance is the key impedance on feed- stocks that could be sustainably used in the mix.

Overall, in order to minimize environmental damage associated with feedstock type in all impact categories considered, and simultaneously maintain a positive energy balance, the analyses suggest that co-digestion of agricultural and food industry residues, and agricultural residues with varying proportions of both MSW and food industry residues (i.e., Base SS-2 and Base LS) are most appropriate for small and large-scale biogas plants, respectively.
57: <header>4.1. Single feedstock digestion scenarios </header>

The LCIA results in Fig. 3 identified agricultural waste and resi- dues (viz., cattle manure, straw), MSW, and food industry residues (viz., food residues, pomace, slaughterhouse waste, grease sepa- rator sludge) as most environmental compatible single feedstocks for biogas production. All were able to reduce the overall envi- ronmental impacts. This observation was mainly attributed to low emissions associated with feedstock handling (Poeschl et al., in press), and marginal impacts of agricultural residues on agricul- tural land occupation (ALO) (Section 3.1.1). It was shown, that the use of straw returned the most attractive environmental perfor- mance, improved by almost 830% compared to the base scenario of cattle manure feedstock.

Comparing the use of energy crop feedstocks for biogas production (viz., corn silage, grass silage, whole wheat plant silage), it was shown that corn silage feedstock was environ- mentally more attractive compared to grass silage or whole wheat plant silage (Fig. 3). For example, for corn silage the damages on ecosystems diversity (ED) were approximately 6 times lower, compared to grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. The ED was mainly influenced by ALO impacts, which strongly differ for the energy crops (Section 3.1.1). ALO impacts for corn silage were equivalent to 20% and 25% of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively (Table 1). This was reflected in overall envi- ronmental reductions for corn silage (-1.4 pt) compared to impacts for grass silage (45.6 pt) and whole wheat plant silage (41.4 pt) (Fig. 3).

Integrated assessment of life-cycle energy performance from individual feedstocks suggested straw and corn silage as the most energy efficient for biogas production (Pöschl et al., 2010). This was mainly ascribed to the higher energy density, which exceeded the primary energy inputs for feedstock supply logistics (Pöschl et al., 2010). Also, the MSW, food residues and slaughterhouse waste feedstocks required pre-treatment/sterilization and collection/ transportation regime prior to utilization as feedstocks in AD process, hence, achieved less attractive energy balances (Pöschl et al., 2010).

With respect to energy balance (Pöschl et al., 2010), the results indicated that co-digestion of cattle manure with corn silage (Base SS), and MSW with food residues (Base LS) represented the most energy efficient co-digestion scenarios, for small and large- scale plants, respectively. Evidence from this study indicates that the environmental performance is the key impedance on feed- stocks that could be sustainably used in the mix.

Overall, in order to minimize environmental damage associated with feedstock type in all impact categories considered, and simultaneously maintain a positive energy balance, the analyses suggest that co-digestion of agricultural and food industry residues, and agricultural residues with varying proportions of both MSW and food industry residues (i.e., Base SS-2 and Base LS) are most appropriate for small and large-scale biogas plants, respectively.
61: <header>4.1. Single feedstock digestion scenarios </header>

The LCIA results in Fig. 3 identified agricultural waste and resi- dues (viz., cattle manure, straw), MSW, and food industry residues (viz., food residues, pomace, slaughterhouse waste, grease sepa- rator sludge) as most environmental compatible single feedstocks for biogas production. All were able to reduce the overall envi- ronmental impacts. This observation was mainly attributed to low emissions associated with feedstock handling (Poeschl et al., in press), and marginal impacts of agricultural residues on agricul- tural land occupation (ALO) (Section 3.1.1). It was shown, that the use of straw returned the most attractive environmental perfor- mance, improved by almost 830% compared to the base scenario of cattle manure feedstock.

Comparing the use of energy crop feedstocks for biogas production (viz., corn silage, grass silage, whole wheat plant silage), it was shown that corn silage feedstock was environ- mentally more attractive compared to grass silage or whole wheat plant silage (Fig. 3). For example, for corn silage the damages on ecosystems diversity (ED) were approximately 6 times lower, compared to grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. The ED was mainly influenced by ALO impacts, which strongly differ for the energy crops (Section 3.1.1). ALO impacts for corn silage were equivalent to 20% and 25% of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively (Table 1). This was reflected in overall envi- ronmental reductions for corn silage (-1.4 pt) compared to impacts for grass silage (45.6 pt) and whole wheat plant silage (41.4 pt) (Fig. 3).

Integrated assessment of life-cycle energy performance from individual feedstocks suggested straw and corn silage as the most energy efficient for biogas production (Pöschl et al., 2010). This was mainly ascribed to the higher energy density, which exceeded the primary energy inputs for feedstock supply logistics (Pöschl et al., 2010). Also, the MSW, food residues and slaughterhouse waste feedstocks required pre-treatment/sterilization and collection/ transportation regime prior to utilization as feedstocks in AD process, hence, achieved less attractive energy balances (Pöschl et al., 2010).

The LCIA results in Fig. 3 identified agricultural waste and resi- dues (viz., cattle manure, straw), MSW, and food industry residues (viz., food residues, pomace, slaughterhouse waste, grease sepa- rator sludge) as most environmental compatible single feedstocks for biogas production. All were able to reduce the overall envi- ronmental impacts. This observation was mainly attributed to low emissions associated with feedstock handling (Poeschl et al., in press), and marginal impacts of agricultural residues on agricul- tural land occupation (ALO) (Section 3.1.1). It was shown, that the use of straw returned the most attractive environmental perfor- mance, improved by almost 830% compared to the base scenario of cattle manure feedstock.

Comparing the use of energy crop feedstocks for biogas production (viz., corn silage, grass silage, whole wheat plant silage), it was shown that corn silage feedstock was environ- mentally more attractive compared to grass silage or whole wheat plant silage (Fig. 3). For example, for corn silage the damages on ecosystems diversity (ED) were approximately 6 times lower, compared to grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. The ED was mainly influenced by ALO impacts, which strongly differ for the energy crops (Section 3.1.1). ALO impacts for corn silage were equivalent to 20% and 25% of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively (Table 1). This was reflected in overall envi- ronmental reductions for corn silage (-1.4 pt) compared to impacts for grass silage (45.6 pt) and whole wheat plant silage (41.4 pt) (Fig. 3).

Integrated assessment of life-cycle energy performance from individual feedstocks suggested straw and corn silage as the most energy efficient for biogas production (Pöschl et al., 2010). This was mainly ascribed to the higher energy density, which exceeded the primary energy inputs for feedstock supply logistics (Pöschl et al., 2010). Also, the MSW, food residues and slaughterhouse waste feedstocks required pre-treatment/sterilization and collection/ transportation regime prior to utilization as feedstocks in AD process, hence, achieved less attractive energy balances (Pöschl et al., 2010).

4.2. Co-digestion of multiple feedstock scenarios

Fig. 4 shows the weighted single-score LCIA results for the multiple feedstock co-digestion scenarios analyzed. It is shown that, for both small and large-scale co-digestion scenarios, co- digestion of mainly agricultural and food industry residues (Base SS-2; -5.2 pt) and MSW with residues of food industry (Base LS-2;

In contrast, co-digestion of cattle manure with high proportion of energy crops exhibited the highest environmental impacts for both small (Base SS-1) and large-scale (Base LS-1) biogas plants. The difference was mainly attributed to feedstock origins; e.g., feed- stocks of predominantly agricultural and food industry residue (Base SS-2) accounted for just 1% of impacts on agricultural land occupation (ALO) compared to the co-digestion of predominantly energy crop feedstock and also reduced the climate change impacts by up to 30% (Table 2). Therefore, to maximize the envi- ronmental impacts mitigation, higher proportion of agricultural residues and organic waste streams should be included in co- digestion scenarios.

The results also show, that co-digestion of corn silage with cattle manure (Base SS), will improve the overall environmental perfor- mance of single feedstock digestion of corn by over 100% (-2.9 pt) (Figs. 3 and 4). Also, the co-digestion of cattle manure with corn silage, grass silage and whole wheat plant silage significantly enhanced the environmental performance for small (Base SS-1) and large-scale (Base LS-1) biogas plants, respectively, compared to single feedstock digestion of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. With respect to energy balance (Pöschl et al., 2010), the results indicated that co-digestion of cattle manure with corn silage (Base SS), and MSW with food residues (Base LS) represented the most energy efficient co-digestion scenarios, for small and large- scale plants, respectively. Evidence from this study indicates that the environmental performance is the key impedance on feed- stocks that could be sustainably used in the mix.

Overall, in order to minimize environmental damage associated with feedstock type in all impact categories considered, and simultaneously maintain a positive energy balance, the analyses suggest that co-digestion of agricultural and food industry residues, and agricultural residues with varying proportions of both MSW and food industry residues (i.e., Base SS-2 and Base LS) are most appropriate for small and large-scale biogas plants, respectively.
66: Fig. 5 shows the weighted single-score LCIA results for biogas utilization scenarios analyzed. For small-scale plants, CHP with

external heat utilization and tri-generation (Base SS-b), were the most environmental friendly. Compared to electricity generation with CHP units (Base SS, Fig. 4), Base SS-b reduced the overall environmental impact by almost 200% (-8.5 pt). The recorded mitigation was mainly attributed to the enhanced efficiency of the combined electricity and thermal energy conversion (Nel = 33%, Nth = 50%, Nel (tri-generation) = 70% waste heat to cooling energy), compared to base case considered (Base SS, Nel = 33%), with commensurate reduction of overall impacts on resources avail- ability (RA), and the substitution of fossil fuel for electricity generation in Germany's fuel mix, and heat generated with natural gas.

The data in Fig. 5 also show that electricity generation using fuel cell coupled with external heat utilization (Base LS-D(U)) could enhance environmental performance by up to 90%, compared to electricity generation in base case (Base LS, Fig. 4).

In comparing the scenarios that included biogas purification and upgrading to biomethane (Base LS-A(U), Base LS-B(U), Base LS- C(U)), it was noted that only the scenario with coupled small- scale CHP unit covering internal heat demands (Base LS-B(U)) was capable of reducing the overall impact on resources avail- ability (RA) by more than double compared to electricity generation alone (Base LS). This was explained by the higher potential for fossil fuel substitution with biomethane, due to higher system efficiency (approximately 100%). However, overall environmental perfor- mance (-1.7 pt) decreased by 60% and 80% compared to electricity generation in CHP (Base LS) and fuel cell with external heat utili- zation (Base LS-D(U)), respectively.

The analysis of biogas utilization pathways from energy conversion efficiency perspective identified the Stirling engine with external heat utilization (Base SS-d), as well as purification and upgrading of biogas to biomethane with coupled small-scale CHP unit (Base LS-B(U)) as the most efficient unit processes in energy conversion for small and large-scale biogas plants, respectively (Pöschl et al., 2010).
Overall, for small and large-scale biogas plants, the most promising pathways for sustainable biogas utilization would be in
Distance to reversal of environmental impact mitigation
|5 km|50 km|100 km|200 km|400 km Cattle manure feedstock|-1.9|-0.5|1.2|4.5|11.1 Corn silage feedstock|-1.4|0.1|1.7|5.0|11.6 MSW feedstock|-3.2|-2.6|-1.9|-0.4|2.4

Fig. 7. Impacts of incremental feedstock transportation distance by feedstock type.

tri-generation (Base SS-b) and purification and upgrading biogas to biomethane coupled with small-scale CHP unit (Base LS-B(U)), respectively. These are underlined by the technical viability of CHP (Base SS-b) compared to Stirling engines (Base SS-d) which can only be deployed in small modular units rated at 30-200 kW (Pöschl et al., 2010). The results indicated that biogas utilization with fuel cell technology (Base LS-D(U)) may be viable both with respect to energy balance (Pöschl et al., 2010) and to miti- gation of environmental emissions (Fig. 5). However, fuel cell technology considered is still at experimental stage and the accurate potential will be clearer after commercialization (Poeschl et al., 2010).

Fig. 6 shows the weighted single-score LCIA results for the waste stream scenarios analyzed. For all damage categories considered, the options that include utilization of residual biogas from digestate storage, were the most environmentally friendly. For example, compared to the scenarios with open digestate storage areas (Base LS-B(W)a, Base LS-C(W)a), the overall envi- ronmental damage could be improved by 470% and 780% for scenarios spreading liquid (Base LS-B(W)b) and solid fraction of digestate (Base LS-C(W)b) on arable land with closed digestate storage area, respectively. For example, in case of composting (Base LS-E(W)), the estimated impacts on human health (HH) and ecosystems diversity (ED) increased by 600% and 200%, respectively (cf. The CC impact category is integrated into HH damage potential, whereas ALO influences ED (Appendix - Table A5).

From the life-cycle energy balance and LCA of various waste stream pathways, the scenarios including covered digestate storage areas (Base LS-B(W)b and Base LS-C(W)b), were identified to be requisite for sustainable digestate management (Pöschl et al., 2010).
4.5. Impacts of feedstock and digestate transportation distances

Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 illustrate the sensitivity of the environmental impacts to potential variation in transportation distances in feed- stock supply and digestate disposal, respectively. It was assumed that dilution effected any feedstock with DM > 12% to enable

|Corn silage- German electricity mix|Corn silage - Electricity from biogas production in CHP|MSW- German electricity mix|MSW Electricity from biogas production in CHP|Food residues German electricity mix|Food residues Electricity from biogas production in CHP Resources availability (RA)|-5.5|-5.8|-1.5|-2.7|-2.4|-3.0 Ecosystems diversity (ED)|5.9|5.8|-0.7|-1.5|-0.8|-1.2 Human Health (HH)|-1.8|-2.1|-0.9|-2.3|-1.3|-1.9 Total (Pt)|-1.4|-2.1|-3.2|-6.5|-4.6|-6.1
Variations in electricity supply source for biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics
Fig. 10. Environmental impacts of electricity supply source for biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics for corn silage, MSW and food residue.

stirring of the digesters so as to maintain uniform conditions (Appendix - Table A1). It was assumed that 12% DM corresponded to 1 tonne of feedstock mixture, e.g., 1 tonne of MSW with 40% DM content was assumed to correspond to 3.3 tonnes of mixture and generating equivalent amount of digestate.

Fig. 7 shows that for net environmental benefit, the maximum transportation distance for cattle manure, corn silage and MSW feedstock should be less than 64, 53 and 229 km, respectively. The comparatively lower value for corn silage reflects the compounded inputs of chemical fertilizers and pesticides, and land use factors accounted in the cultivation process (Ecoinvent v2.1 database), in contrast to cattle manure where only transportation was required. In the related study (Pöschl et al., 2010), energy balances were assessed to be negative for cattle manure, corn silage and MSW transportation distances in excess of 22, 345 and 425 km, respec- tively. The results suggest that MSW is most ideal where feedstock procurement involves long transportation distance.

With respect to digestate disposal (Fig. 8), for net environmental benefit, the maximum transportation distance for disposal of digestate from cattle manure, corn silage and MSW feedstocks should be within 95, 19 and 33 km radius, respectively. Digestate separation, loading, transport and spreading on arable land nor- mally influence the energy inputs for digestate processing and handling. However, these differences in environmental impacts of digestate disposal were mainly based on values of active fertilizer ingredients, hence, value as mineral fertilizing substitute. For example, cattle manure shows 10, 7 and 3 times higher active fertilizer ingredients in P2O5 (triple superphosphate fertilizers), K2O (potassium sulfate) and NH4-N (ammonium nitrate) per tonne of dry digestate, respectively, compared to corn silage (Poeschl et al., in press).

With respect to digestate disposal (Fig. 8), for net environmental benefit, the maximum transportation distance for disposal of digestate from cattle manure, corn silage and MSW feedstocks should be within 95, 19 and 33 km radius, respectively. Digestate separation, loading, transport and spreading on arable land nor- mally influence the energy inputs for digestate processing and handling. However, these differences in environmental impacts of digestate disposal were mainly based on values of active fertilizer ingredients, hence, value as mineral fertilizing substitute. For example, cattle manure shows 10, 7 and 3 times higher active fertilizer ingredients in P2O5 (triple superphosphate fertilizers), K2O (potassium sulfate) and NH4-N (ammonium nitrate) per tonne of dry digestate, respectively, compared to corn silage (Poeschl et al., in press).

76: <header>4.6. Impact of variation in biogas yield for MSW feedstock </header>

It would be expected that the composition of MSW is highly variable and can influence the biogas yield by up to +10% compared to the average yield of 123 m³ that was assumed in the MSW analyses (Wiljan, 2009). Fig. 9 shows a negative linear correlation of environmental damages with the biogas yield within the specified range. From this data, it was estimated that the total environmental impact of biogas production from MSW feedstock, will vary by about 0.075 Pt per unit percentage point variation in biogas yield.
4.7. Impact of electricity supply source for biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics

Fig. 10 shows the influence on environmental impacts with variation in electricity supply source for biogas plant operation and feedstock supply logistics in AD for corn silage, MSW and food residues feedstocks. Utilization of industry waste, MSW and food waste considered energy intensive pre-treatment and/or sterilization process before qualifying to be used as AD feedstock (Pöschl et al., 2010). It was assumed that electricity was provided by Germany's fuel mix or in contrast by biogas plant's own electricity generated by CHP unit. It was shown that mitigation of environmental impacts between 35% (food residues, -6.1 pt) and 100% (MSW, -6.5 pt) could be reached using the biogas plant's own electricity utilized in CHP unit compared to elec- tricity supply from Germany's fuel mix. Most significant improvements were realized for MSW and food residues, as electricity input for pre-treatment and sterilization and 24 kWhel t-1 could be provided from renewable source (Pöschl et al., 2010).

78: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) was conducted to compare different biogas systems to establish the basis for further improvement of environmental and public health impacts, hence, enhance envi- ronmental sustainability of biogas production and utilization path- ways. Impacts of realistic case scenarios were analyzed by considering potential variations in; (i) feedstock type, (ii) biogas utilization options, and (iii) digestate processing and handling unit processes. The observed range of variations on the potential reduction of environmental impacts (established by LCA) and enhancement of energy conversion efficiency (established by energy balance) indicated a high inherent optimization potential based on judicious selection of biogas production and utilization pathways.
On the basis of the LCA and life-cycle energy balance conducted, the following conclusions may be drawn

<list>(1) In single feedstock digestion, compounded LCA and life-cycle energy analyses indicated that straw and corn silage were sustainable feedstocks for biogas production, due to their higher energy density compounded with comparatively low environ- mental impacts associated with feedstock supply logistics and land use. For example, corn silage achieved up to 6 times lower damages to ecosystem diversity compared to grass silage and whole wheat plant silage. This was mainly influenced by agri- cultural land occupation impacts, equivalent to 20% and 25% of grass silage and whole wheat plant silage, respectively. 
(2) Results in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks showed that sustainable energy balance is achievable both with energy crop-agricultural residue and MSW-food residue feedstock mixtures, which provides a basis for feedstock flexibility. However, feedstock mixtures of predominantly agricultural and food industry residues accounted for just 1% of impacts on agricultural land occupation, compared to the co-digestion of predominantly energy crop feedstock. Therefore, to maximize the environmental impacts mitigation, higher proportion of agricultural residues and organic waste streams should be included in co-digestion. 
(3) The most viable pathway for sustainable biogas utilization in small and large-scale biogas plants were, in tri-generation (CHP + cooling) and purification and upgrading biogas to biomethane coupled with small-scale CHP unit, respectively. In the case of biogas utilization pathways involving small-scale plants, overall environmental performance could be improved by almost 200% by external heat utilization from the coupling CHP unit and tri-generation, due to enhanced combined electricity and thermal conversion efficiency. The results from this investigation indicated that biogas utilization with fuel cell technology is viable both with respect to energy balance and environmental impacts. 
(4) Recovery of residual biogas from the digestate storage areas was the key determinant of the performance of digestate processing and handling. For instance, overall environmental impacts could be improved by ca. tenfold, compared to respective scenarios with open digestate storage area. The main factor was in the reduced biogas leakage to the atmosphere and commensurate replacement of fossil fuel used in energy generation. 
(5) Overall, considering the variations in the feedstock-to-biogas energy conversion efficiency and related environmental impacts of path-specific scenarios as well as variations in transportation distances for feedstock and digestate analyzed in this study, judicious selection of feedstock resources and biogas conversion and utilization are crucial to sustainable biogas deployment.</list>

(2) Results in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks showed that sustainable energy balance is achievable both with energy crop-agricultural residue and MSW-food residue feedstock mixtures, which provides a basis for feedstock flexibility. However, feedstock mixtures of predominantly agricultural and food industry residues accounted for just 1% of impacts on agricultural land occupation, compared to the co-digestion of predominantly energy crop feedstock. Therefore, to maximize the environmental impacts mitigation, higher proportion of agricultural residues and organic waste streams should be included in co-digestion. 
(3) The most viable pathway for sustainable biogas utilization in small and large-scale biogas plants were, in tri-generation (CHP + cooling) and purification and upgrading biogas to biomethane coupled with small-scale CHP unit, respectively. In the case of biogas utilization pathways involving small-scale plants, overall environmental performance could be improved by almost 200% by external heat utilization from the coupling CHP unit and tri-generation, due to enhanced combined electricity and thermal conversion efficiency. The results from this investigation indicated that biogas utilization with fuel cell technology is viable both with respect to energy balance and environmental impacts. 
(4) Recovery of residual biogas from the digestate storage areas was the key determinant of the performance of digestate processing and handling. For instance, overall environmental impacts could be improved by ca. tenfold, compared to respective scenarios with open digestate storage area. The main factor was in the reduced biogas leakage to the atmosphere and commensurate replacement of fossil fuel used in energy generation. 
(5) Overall, considering the variations in the feedstock-to-biogas energy conversion efficiency and related environmental impacts of path-specific scenarios as well as variations in transportation distances for feedstock and digestate analyzed in this study, judicious selection of feedstock resources and biogas conversion and utilization are crucial to sustainable biogas deployment.</list>

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

a Biogas yield in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks was assumed to be 10% higher, than in single feedstock digestion. Energy derived from biogas substitutes fossil electricity from Germany's fuel mix and natural gas based heating; bio- methane substitutes natural gas or diesel fuel in the transportation sector (Poeschl et al., in press). Large-scale biogas systems typically produce >500 kWel in contrast to small-scale biogas systems with typically kWe1.
b Approximately 75% of biogas systems in the Federal State of Bavaria/Germany use cattle manure in co-digestion for biogas production with an average proportion of 55% (FAL, 2005).
C Approximately 80% of biogas systems in Germany use corn silage in co-digestion for biogas production (FAL, 2005), resulting from existing renewable energy resources incentive under the EEG.
d Data based on (BioEnergieService Aschheim GmbH, 2009; Bio, 2001; FvB, 2008; Kübler et al., 2000).

<list>a In endpoint analysis impacts on climate change are divided into climate change human health and climate change ecosystems. 
b DALY = Disability-Adjusted Loss of Life Years; species.yr = loss of species during a year due to environmental impacts; $ = increased costs for future extractions (Heijungs et al., 2003).

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

a Biogas yield in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks was assumed to be 10% higher, than in single feedstock digestion. Energy derived from biogas substitutes fossil electricity from Germany's fuel mix and natural gas based heating; bio- methane substitutes natural gas or diesel fuel in the transportation sector (Poeschl et al., in press). Large-scale biogas systems typically produce >500 kWel in contrast to small-scale biogas systems with typically kWe1.
b Approximately 75% of biogas systems in the Federal State of Bavaria/Germany use cattle manure in co-digestion for biogas production with an average proportion of 55% (FAL, 2005).
C Approximately 80% of biogas systems in Germany use corn silage in co-digestion for biogas production (FAL, 2005), resulting from existing renewable energy resources incentive under the EEG.
d Data based on (BioEnergieService Aschheim GmbH, 2009; Bio, 2001; FvB, 2008; Kübler et al., 2000).

<list>a In endpoint analysis impacts on climate change are divided into climate change human health and climate change ecosystems. 
b DALY = Disability-Adjusted Loss of Life Years; species.yr = loss of species during a year due to environmental impacts; $ = increased costs for future extractions (Heijungs et al., 2003).

He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

a Biogas yield in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks was assumed to be 10% higher, than in single feedstock digestion. Energy derived from biogas substitutes fossil electricity from Germany's fuel mix and natural gas based heating; bio- methane substitutes natural gas or diesel fuel in the transportation sector (Poeschl et al., in press). Large-scale biogas systems typically produce >500 kWel in contrast to small-scale biogas systems with typically kWe1.
b Approximately 75% of biogas systems in the Federal State of Bavaria/Germany use cattle manure in co-digestion for biogas production with an average proportion of 55% (FAL, 2005).

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

a Biogas yield in co-digestion of multiple feedstocks was assumed to be 10% higher, than in single feedstock digestion. Energy derived from biogas substitutes fossil electricity from Germany's fuel mix and natural gas based heating; bio- methane substitutes natural gas or diesel fuel in the transportation sector (Poeschl et al., in press). Large-scale biogas systems typically produce >500 kWel in contrast to small-scale biogas systems with typically kWe1.

<list>a In endpoint analysis impacts on climate change are divided into climate change human health and climate change ecosystems. 
b DALY = Disability-Adjusted Loss of Life Years; species.yr = loss of species during a year due to environmental impacts; $ = increased costs for future extractions (Heijungs et al., 2003).

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.

Large-scale biogas systems typically produce >500 kWel in contrast to small-scale biogas systems with typically kWe1.

He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. <list>a In endpoint analysis impacts on climate change are divided into climate change human health and climate change ecosystems. 
b DALY = Disability-Adjusted Loss of Life Years; species.yr = loss of species during a year due to environmental impacts; $ = increased costs for future extractions (Heijungs et al., 2003). 
C The characterized results are divided by the impact of a European citizen during one year for all impact categories (person*yr). Normalized results are dimensionless. 
d 
The hierarchist perspective (average weighting set), which weights the human health, ecosystems diversity and resources availability damage categories in the ratio of 4:4:2 was used to weight the normalized values to evaluate the single-score final impact value for the systems, expressed as eco-points.</list>

Amsterdam, Netherlands.|Shane Ward is Professor of Biosystems Engineering, and Head of School of Agriculture, Food Science & Veterinary Medicine at University College Dublin. He was founder and director of Bioresources Research Centre (BRC) at the university, with responsibility for a large research group dealing with a variety of aspects of the agri-food and Bio- resource industries. He was formerly Head of Production Research at the R&D Depart- ment of Bord na Mona (Irish Peat Board). Prof. Ward is Fellow of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland and has over 28 years of experience in research and technology development. He has established several research units some of which are in partner- ships with industry. A former Chairperson of its Agri-Food Division of the Institution of Engineers of Ireland, he was responsible for the promotion and development of the agri-food and bioresource industry among professional engineers and the industry. He has had a strong track record in cooperation projects that include collaboration with industry. Prof. Ward has in excess of 150 peer-reviewed research publications, and has supervised in excess of 40 Masters and PhD students. His current research
Helm, M., 2009. Degradation Degree of Organic Dry Matter of Single Feedstock to Determine Dry Matter Content of Its Digestate. Dr. Markus Helm GmbH -
team comprises approximately 30 researchers (Masters, PhDs and Post-Doctoral fellows), working on a variety of bioresource projects.
Philip Owende is a senior research fellow in the Charles Parsons Energy Research Programme of Science Foundation Ireland, University College Dublin. His research

area of interest is in biomass-to-energy systems covering energy feedstock production, pre-treatment standards, conversion technologies, and environmental impact assessment. Dr. Owende has published 40 scientific articles in peer- reviewed journals, over 30 international conference papers, and three book chapters.
